abstract: Cotraining with demonstration data generated both in simulation and on real hardware has emerged as a promising recipe for scaling imitation learning in robotics. This work seeks to elucidate basic principles of this sim-and-real cotraining to inform simulation design, sim-and-real dataset creation, and policy training. Our experiments confirm that cotraining with simulated data can dramatically improve performance, especially when real data is limited. We show that these performance gains scale with additional simulated data up to a plateau; adding more real-world data increases this performance ceiling. The results also suggest that reducing physical domain gaps may be more impactful than visual fidelity for non-prehensile or contact-rich tasks. Perhaps surprisingly, we find that some visual gap can help cotraining -- binary probes reveal that high-performing policies must learn to distinguish simulated domains from real. We conclude by investigating this nuance and mechanisms that facilitate positive transfer between sim-and-real. Focusing narrowly on the canonical task of planar pushing from pixels allows us to be thorough in our study. In total, our experiments span 50+ real-world policies (evaluated on 1000+ trials) and 250 simulated policies (evaluated on 50,000+ trials). Videos and code can be found at this https URL.

keyInsights: Policies learn to discern between simulated and real environments, and this domain awareness is crucial for effective action prediction, especially when underlying physics differ.
Simulated data facilitates positive transfer by improving dataset coverage, allowing policies to utilize sim-learned behaviors for states underrepresented in real data.

The scaling of performance with simulated data follows predictable power-law relationships, but scaling real data generally yields larger exponents, indicating its greater impact on the performance ceiling.
originalProblem: The prohibitive cost and time of collecting real-world robot data impede scaling imitation learning compared to other AI fields.
Difficulty in effectively leveraging easily scalable simulated data with expensive real-world data to improve robot policy performance.
Lack of foundational understanding of the underlying mechanisms and optimal strategies for sim-and-real cotraining in robotics.
results: Cotraining dramatically improved real-world policy performance, achieving 2-7x improvement with limited real data and increasing success rates from 10/20 to 19/20 for $|D_R|=50$.
Physical accuracy in simulation is paramount for contact-rich tasks, with even small physics gaps leading to significant performance degradation.

Data mixing directly (cotraining) consistently outperformed pretraining-finetuning strategies, and optimal mixing ratios (alpha) were critical for maximizing performance, especially with scarce real data.
solution: Empirically analyzed sim-and-real cotraining for Diffusion Policies on a planar pushing task, jointly training policies on mixed real and simulated data.
Conducted extensive experiments across various real-world data sizes, simulated data scales, and mixing ratios (alpha) using a KUKA LBR iiwa 7 robot and a 'sim-and-sim' setup.
Systematically investigated the impact of different sim2real distribution shifts (visual, physics, task gaps) and the mechanisms of positive transfer through embedding analysis and scaling laws.
paper_summary.summary: An empirical study from MIT explored sim-and-real cotraining of diffusion policies for planar pushing, showing it substantially boosts robot policy performance with limited real data. The analysis highlighted the critical role of physical simulation accuracy and the benefit of policies distinguishing between real and simulated domains for optimal action prediction.

title: Empirical Analysis of Sim-and-Real Cotraining of Diffusion Policies for Planar Pushing from Pixels

abstract: Robotic loco-manipulation tasks often involve contact-rich interactions with the environment, requiring the joint modeling of contact force and robot position. However, recent visuomotor policies often focus solely on learning position or force control, overlooking their co-learning. In this work, we propose the first unified policy for legged robots that jointly models force and position control learned without reliance on force sensors. By simulating diverse combinations of position and force commands alongside external disturbance forces, we use reinforcement learning to learn a policy that estimates forces from historical robot states and compensates for them through position and velocity adjustments. This policy enables a wide range of manipulation behaviors under varying force and position inputs, including position tracking, force application, force tracking, and compliant interactions. Furthermore, we demonstrate that the learned policy enhances trajectory-based imitation learning pipelines by incorporating essential contact information through its force estimation module, achieving approximately 39.5% higher success rates across four challenging contact-rich manipulation tasks compared to position-control policies. Extensive experiments on both a quadrupedal manipulator and a humanoid robot validate the versatility and robustness of the proposed policy across diverse scenarios.

organization_info.name: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications
Beijing Institute for General Artificial Intelligence (BIGAI)

keyInsights: A single learned policy can effectively manage both position and force control for legged robots, enabling a wide range of compliant and interactive behaviors without explicit force sensors.
Contact forces can be robustly estimated from a robot's proprioceptive states alone, offering a hardware-agnostic solution for force awareness in legged loco-manipulation.
Augmenting imitation learning datasets with estimated contact force information significantly boosts the success rate of policies in complex, contact-rich real-world manipulation tasks.
originalProblem: Legged robots struggle with complex, contact-rich loco-manipulation due to their intricate kinematics and the need for precise force control.
The absence of direct force-sensing hardware on many legged robots limits their capacity for compliant and nuanced physical interaction.
Traditional imitation learning methods rely on datasets lacking contact force information, hindering performance in tasks requiring physical interaction.
results: The unified policy accurately tracks end-effector position (error within 0.1m in simulation) and applies commanded forces (average error within 10N in real-world experiments).
Force-aware imitation learning achieved approximately 39.5% higher success rates on challenging real-world contact-rich tasks like wiping and cabinet opening compared to position-only baselines.
The policy demonstrated strong generalizability, supporting diverse manipulation behaviors and performing effectively on both quadrupedal (Unitree B2-Z1) and humanoid (Unitree G1) robot platforms.
solution: A unified force-position control policy is developed using reinforcement learning in simulation, leveraging an impedance control formulation for both end-effector and base.
A learned state estimator is integrated into the policy to predict external contact forces from proprioceptive states, thus eliminating the need for physical force sensors.
The learned policy is used to create a novel "force-aware" data collection pipeline, enriching imitation learning datasets with estimated contact forces for improved downstream performance.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at BIGAI and UniTree Robotics developed a unified policy for legged robots to control both position and contact force without relying on external force sensors. This policy enhanced imitation learning by generating force-aware data, leading to a 39.5% improvement in success rates for contact-rich manipulation tasks on quadrupedal and humanoid robots.

title: Learning a Unified Policy for Position and Force Control in Legged Loco-Manipulation

abstract: The specification of the action space plays a pivotal role in imitation-based robotic manipulation policy learning, fundamentally shaping the optimization landscape of policy learning. While recent advances have focused heavily on scaling training data and model capacity, the choice of action space remains guided by ad-hoc heuristics or legacy designs, leading to an ambiguous understanding of robotic policy design philosophies. To address this ambiguity, we conducted a large-scale and systematic empirical study, confirming that the action space does have significant and complex impacts on robotic policy learning. We dissect the action design space along temporal and spatial axes, facilitating a structured analysis of how these choices govern both policy learnability and control stability. Based on 13,000+ real-world rollouts on a bimanual robot and evaluation on 500+ trained models over four scenarios, we examine the trade-offs between absolute vs. delta representations, and joint-space vs. task-space parameterizations. Our large-scale results suggest that properly designing the policy to predict delta actions consistently improves performance, while joint-space and task-space representations offer complementary strengths, favoring control stability and generalization, respectively.

organization_info.name: Institute for AI Industry Research (AIR), Tsinghua University

keyInsights: Temporal abstraction critically affects control stability, with chunk-wise delta actions mitigating noise amplification inherent in step-wise integration, ensuring error bounds remain constant.

Delta-based action representations offer a more tractable and locally coherent learning target, consistently outperforming absolute representations by enabling policies to focus on immediate displacements.

The optimal spatial abstraction is contingent on the objective: joint-space control captures robot kinematics effectively with powerful generative models and sufficient data, while task-space control excels in transfer learning due to its embodiment-invariant nature.
originalProblem: The robotics community lacks a unified understanding and consensus on optimal action space design, leading to ad-hoc choices and inconsistent practices.
The impact of action space choices on policy learnability, control stability, reproducibility, and cross-embodiment generalization has not been systematically studied.
Developing generalist robot models is hindered by the fragmentation and unsystematic approaches to representing robot actions.
results: Chunk-wise delta actions consistently and significantly outperformed step-wise delta, supported by a theoretical analysis showing reduced noise amplification and exhibiting abstraction-dependent optimal execution horizons.
Delta-based action representations showed consistent and significant performance improvements over absolute actions across all robotic platforms, manipulation tasks, and model variations.

Joint-space control demonstrated overall stronger performance, especially when paired with powerful generative models and increased data, while task-space control achieved a pronounced advantage in cross-embodiment and transfer learning scenarios with foundation models.
solution: The authors defined a comprehensive taxonomy for action representation, categorizing actions by temporal (absolute, delta, chunking variants) and spatial (joint-space, task-space) abstractions.

A large-scale empirical study was conducted, involving over 2,000 demonstrations and 13,000 real-world rollouts across multiple robotic platforms (single-arm, dual-arm, different morphologies) and a high-fidelity simulation environment.

Policies were evaluated using various learning paradigms (regression-based, flow matching-based, foundation models) and under different conditions of data scale, training duration, and execution horizons.
paper_summary.summary: This empirical study systematically investigates action space design for robotic manipulation, establishing principled guidelines for policy learnability and control stability. It demonstrates that chunk-wise delta actions reduce cumulative errors, delta representations improve learning efficiency, and task-space actions enhance transferability for generalist robot models across various tasks and platforms.

title: Demystifying Action Space Design for Robotic Manipulation Policies

abstract:
Reinforcement learning (RL) has made significant strides in legged robot
control, enabling locomotion across diverse terrains and complex
loco-manipulation capabilities. However, the commonly used position or velocity
tracking-based objectives are agnostic to forces experienced by the robot,
leading to stiff and potentially dangerous behaviors and poor control during
forceful interactions. To address this limitation, we present
\emph{Force-Adaptive Control via Impedance Reference Tracking} (FACET).
Inspired by impedance control, we use RL to train a control policy to imitate a
virtual mass-spring-damper system, allowing fine-grained control under external
forces by manipulating the virtual spring. In simulation, we demonstrate that
our quadruped robot achieves improved robustness to large impulses (up to 200
Ns) and exhibits controllable compliance, achieving an 80% reduction in
collision impulse. The policy is deployed to a physical robot to showcase both
compliance and the ability to engage with large forces by kinesthetic control
and pulling payloads up to 2/3 of its weight. Further extension to a legged
loco-manipulator and a humanoid shows the applicability of our method to more
complex settings to enable whole-body compliance control. Project Website:
this https URL

keyInsights: Learning to imitate the integrated position and velocity trajectories of a virtual impedance model provides a more stable and effective approach to force-adaptive control for legged robots than tracking raw accelerations.

Temporal smoothing of tracking targets is crucial for generating robust and learnable reward signals, especially for student policies operating with imperfect state estimation in real-world scenarios.

A two-stage teacher-student reinforcement learning architecture, combined with extensive domain randomization, effectively bridges the sim-to-real gap, allowing complex force-adaptive behaviors to be learned in simulation and deployed on physical robots.
originalProblem: Most existing reinforcement learning (RL) controllers for legged robots are 'force-agnostic', leading to stiff behaviors that can be dangerous during physical interactions and provide poor control for tasks requiring force exertion.
Applying traditional impedance control directly to legged robots is challenging due to their complex, contact-rich dynamics and difficulties in precisely measuring or tracking ground reaction forces.

Prior RL efforts for compliant or force-controlled legged robots often lack direct task-space compliance or require explicit mode switching, limiting their versatility and safety in dynamic environments.
results: FACET-controlled robots demonstrated superior robustness, withstanding large lateral impulses up to 200 Ns by exhibiting compliant 'following' behavior, significantly outperforming baseline controllers that became unstable.

The framework enabled controllable compliance, achieving substantially lower collision impulses during interactions with obstacles and allowing for modulation of compliance by tuning the virtual stiffness parameter.

On a real Unitree Go2 quadruped, the policy enabled effortless compliant following via kinesthetic guidance and successfully pulled payloads up to 10 kg (approximately two-thirds of its own weight), significantly surpassing the capabilities of built-in and robust velocity-tracking baselines.
solution: A reinforcement learning policy is trained to track the integrated position and velocity trajectories of a virtual mass-spring-damper system defined for the robot's Center of Mass (CoM), providing an indirect force commanding interface.
Temporal smoothing is introduced to the tracking targets, where the reward signal uses a mixture of targets integrated from multiple past time steps, ensuring a stable and robust learning process.

A two-stage teacher-student training framework is employed; a teacher policy uses privileged simulation information, and a student policy is finetuned with observable information and a learned state estimator for robust sim-to-real transfer.
paper_summary.summary: FACET is a reinforcement learning framework that enables legged robots to achieve force-adaptive control and tunable compliance by learning to imitate a virtual mass-spring-damper system. This approach allows robots to gracefully manage external forces, perform high-force interaction tasks, and demonstrates improved robustness against perturbations, successfully pulling payloads up to 10 kg on a real quadruped robot.

title: FACET: Force-Adaptive Control via Impedance Reference Tracking for  Legged Robots

abstract:
Generalizing control policies to novel embodiments remains a fundamental
challenge in enabling scalable and transferable learning in robotics. While
prior works have explored this in locomotion, a systematic study in the context
of manipulation tasks remains limited, partly due to the lack of standardized
benchmarks. In this paper, we introduce a benchmark for learning
cross-embodiment manipulation, focusing on two foundational tasks-reach and
push-across a diverse range of morphologies. The benchmark is designed to test
generalization along three axes: interpolation (testing performance within a
robot category that shares the same link structure), extrapolation (testing on
a robot with a different link structure), and composition (testing on
combinations of link structures). On the benchmark, we evaluate the ability of
different RL policies to learn from multiple morphologies and to generalize to
novel ones. Our study aims to answer whether morphology-aware training can
outperform single-embodiment baselines, whether zero-shot generalization to
unseen morphologies is feasible, and how consistently these patterns hold
across different generalization regimes. The results highlight the current
limitations of multi-embodiment learning and provide insights into how
architectural and training design choices influence policy generalization.

keyInsights: Multi-embodiment (ME) training can achieve comparable or even superior multi-task performance on *seen* robots compared to single-embodiment policies, benefiting from a regularizing effect leading to smoother learning.
Zero-shot generalization is feasible and strong for interpolation tasks, where agents adapt to geometric variations within known morphological categories.

However, zero-shot generalization remains a significant challenge for extrapolation and composition tasks, indicating that current methods struggle to reason about entirely novel link structures or novel combinations of components.
originalProblem: A lack of standardized, comprehensive benchmarks for systematically evaluating cross-embodiment learning in robotic manipulation tasks.
The inability of current robotic control policies to generalize effectively to novel physical embodiments, requiring extensive retraining for each new robot.

Existing large-scale datasets often confound morphological generalization with complex perception challenges, while simulation benchmarks typically feature limited robot morphological variation and focus on locomotion over manipulation.
results: ME-Transformer agents consistently achieved comparable or superior multi-task scores on training morphologies and demonstrated strong zero-shot generalization for interpolation tasks, often matching or surpassing SE baselines.

For extrapolation and composition tasks, ME agents showed low success rates (e.g., 0% for `Arms-push` in extrapolation), lagging far behind SE baselines, highlighting a critical failure in generalizing to truly novel morphologies.

The adoption of `symlog` returns and discrete action spaces significantly boosted ME-Transformer performance (e.g., 4x multi-task, 2x zero-shot for `Arm3 reach`), proving crucial for stable and effective learning in multi-embodiment settings.
solution: Introduced `AnyBody`, a benchmark suite within NVIDIA Isaac-Sim featuring 18 diverse robot variations (procedural and real-world) and two fundamental manipulation tasks (reach and push).
Defined clear generalization axes: interpolation (geometric variations within a category), extrapolation (fundamentally different link structures), and composition (novel component combinations).

Evaluated multi-embodiment (ME) policies, including Transformer-based architectures, against single-embodiment (SE) baselines, incorporating stable reinforcement learning techniques like symlog returns and discrete action spaces for precise tasks.
paper_summary.summary: Princeton University researchers developed "AnyBody," a benchmark suite for cross-embodiment manipulation, revealing that while multi-embodiment policies can achieve competitive performance on seen robots and interpolation tasks, they largely fail at zero-shot generalization to entirely novel or compositionally different robot structures. This highlights the limitations of current methods in achieving broad morphological adaptability for manipulation.

title: AnyBody: A Benchmark Suite for Cross-Embodiment Manipulation

abstract: We study the choice of action space in robot manipulation learning and sim-to-real transfer. We define metrics that assess the performance, and examine the emerging properties in the different action spaces. We train over 250 reinforcement learning~(RL) agents in simulated reaching and pushing tasks, using 13 different control spaces. The choice of spaces spans combinations of common action space design characteristics. We evaluate the training performance in simulation and the transfer to a real-world environment. We identify good and bad characteristics of robotic action spaces and make recommendations for future designs. Our findings have important implications for the design of RL algorithms for robot manipulation tasks, and highlight the need for careful consideration of action spaces when training and transferring RL agents for real-world robotics.

keyInsights: The choice of action space significantly influences both the efficiency of learning in simulation and the safety and smoothness of policies when transferred to a real robot.
Action spaces that control higher-order derivatives (e.g., velocity-based) and inherently limit normalized tracking error (NTE) generally facilitate more effective sim-to-real transfer.
Simulation success rates are not direct indicators of real-world performance, highlighting the need for action space designs that specifically address transfer challenges.
originalProblem: Deploying reinforcement learning for robot manipulation in the real world is challenging due to safety concerns and high exploration costs.
The 'sim-to-real gap' remains incompletely understood, with existing research primarily focusing on state/observation spaces rather than the action space's influence.
A systematic analysis of how various action space designs impact learning efficiency and real-world transferability was lacking.
results: Joint Velocity (JV) action spaces, including their delta variants, demonstrated superior sim-to-real transfer capabilities, exhibiting low offline trajectory error (OTE), high real-world task accuracy (ACC), and low expected constraint violations (ECV).
Cartesian action spaces showed an advantage in sample efficiency and reward for the contact-rich pushing task, while velocity-based controls generally outperformed position-based ones in simulation.

Joint Torque (JT) control proved unsafe and unreliable for real-world contact tasks, and multi-step integrator (MI∆) delta action spaces generally performed less effectively in terms of the sim-to-real gap compared to base or one-step integrator (OI∆) variants.
solution: The authors conducted a large-scale empirical study, training over 250 Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) agents across two manipulation tasks (reaching and pushing) on a 7-DOF Franka Emika Panda robot.
Thirteen distinct action space variants were systematically evaluated, covering joint vs. Cartesian control, position vs. velocity vs. torque commands, and one-step vs. multi-step delta formulations.
Performance was assessed using metrics for training efficiency, policy usability (expected constraint violations), sim-to-real gap (offline trajectory error), and real-world task success rates.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at the Volkswagen Group, Machine Learning Research Lab, empirically investigated how robot action space design affects reinforcement learning and sim-to-real transfer for manipulation tasks. Their study of 13 action space variants revealed that Joint Velocity (JV) control consistently offers the most robust transfer performance, characterized by low offline trajectory error, high real-world accuracy, and minimal constraint violations.

title: On the Role of the Action Space in Robot Manipulation Learning and Sim-to-Real Transfer

abstract: We investigate what specific design choices enable successful online reinforcement learning (RL) on physical robots. Across 100 real-world training runs on three distinct robotic platforms, we systematically ablate algorithmic, systems, and experimental decisions that are typically left implicit in prior work. We find that some widely used defaults can be harmful, while a set of robust, readily adopted design choices within standard RL practice yield stable learning across tasks and hardware. These results provide the first large-sample empirical study of such design choices, enabling practitioners to deploy online RL with lower engineering effort.

keyInsights: Effectively scaling off-policy algorithms in simulation for robust sim-to-real transfer requires a high update-to-data ratio and a large number of domain-randomized environments (N_e ~ 10^3).

Stabilizing online fine-tuning on real robots relies heavily on practical data management strategies, such as retaining simulation data and previous online data within the replay buffer, and utilizing warm starts.

Asymmetric actor-critic updates, specifically delaying actor updates and using a reduced actor learning rate, are critical for preventing policy divergence and ensuring stable learning in the presence of distribution shifts.
originalProblem: Transferring reinforcement learning policies trained in simulation to real robots often faces a "sim-to-real gap," making online adaptation on physical hardware challenging.
Online reinforcement learning on real robots is frequently unstable and sample-inefficient, often leading to a "downward spiral" where fine-tuning degrades performance.
Existing deep RL applications in robotics often rely on customized setups or lack systematic empirical studies of practical deployment challenges across diverse platforms.
results: The study demonstrated that retaining simulation data and previous real-world data significantly improved learning efficiency and stability, often by acting as a regularizer during online fine-tuning.
Warm-starting the online replay buffer with initial real-world data collected by the prior policy was shown to improve learning stability for locomotion and navigation tasks.

Asymmetric actor-critic updates, updating the actor less frequently and with a smaller learning rate, were essential for preventing performance degradation and enabling stable online policy improvement across all tested robotic platforms.
solution: A systematic empirical study was conducted, involving over 100 real-world training runs on three distinct robotic platforms (manipulation, locomotion, navigation), using standard off-policy RL algorithms like SAC.
Key design choices were investigated: retaining simulation or past real-world data in the replay buffer, using warm starts to pre-fill the buffer, and implementing asymmetric actor-critic updates.
An open-source training pipeline and robotic stack for the Franka Emika Panda were provided to facilitate reproducibility and lower entry barriers for real-world RL experimentation.
paper_summary.summary: This paper empirically investigates crucial design choices for successfully fine-tuning simulation-trained reinforcement learning policies on real robots in an online setting. It demonstrates that standard off-policy algorithms can achieve stable performance on diverse robotic platforms by employing techniques like data retention, warm starts, and asymmetric actor-critic updates.

title: What Matters for Simulation to Online Reinforcement Learning on Real Robots

abstract: Sim-to-real transfer for contact-rich manipulation remains challenging due to the inherent discrepancy in contact dynamics. While existing methods often rely on costly real-world data or utilize blind compliance through fixed controllers, we propose a framework that leverages expert-designed controller logic for transfer. Inspired by the success of privileged supervision in kinematic tasks, we employ a human-designed finite state machine based position/force controller in simulation to provide privileged guidance. The resulting policy is trained to predict the end-effector pose, contact state, and crucially the desired contact force direction. Unlike force magnitudes, which are highly sensitive to simulation inaccuracies, force directions encode high-level task geometry and remain robust across the sim-to-real gap. At deployment, these predictions configure a force-aware admittance controller. By combining the policy's directional intent with a constant, low-cost manually tuned force magnitude, the system generates adaptive, task-aligned compliance. This tuning is lightweight, typically requiring only a single scalar per contact state. We provide theoretical analysis for stability and robustness to disturbances. Experiments on four real-world tasks, i.e., microwave opening, peg-in-hole, whiteboard wiping, and door opening, demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms strong baselines in both success rate and robustness. Videos are available at: this https URL.

keyInsights: The *direction* of the desired contact force is primarily constrained by task geometry and remains robust across the sim-to-real gap, unlike force *magnitudes* which are highly sensitive to simulation inaccuracies.

Decoupling the prediction of force direction (from simulation) from the application of force magnitude (via a simple, real-world scalar tune) effectively bridges the sim-to-real gap for contact-rich tasks.

Intelligent, adaptive compliance, achieved by dynamically adjusting control parameters based on predicted task-relevant force directions and contact states, significantly improves robustness and precision compared to fixed, isotropic compliance.
originalProblem: Policies trained in simulation for contact-rich tasks often fail in the real world due to discrepancies in physical dynamics and contact models (the "sim-to-real dynamics gap").
Learning directly in the real world for contact-rich tasks is costly, time-consuming, and carries safety risks, limiting scalability.

Existing sim-to-real methods often rely on position-only policies with fixed, isotropic compliant controllers, leading to "blind compliance" that passively accommodates errors rather than intelligently managing force interactions.
results: The method achieved an overall success rate of 91% across four diverse contact-rich tasks (Microwave Opening, Peg-in-Hole, Whiteboard Wiping, Door Opening), significantly outperforming the best baseline (E2VLA+M-A) at 67%.
It substantially reduced both "Insufficient Force" and "Excessive Force" failures compared to baselines, demonstrating the ability to apply appropriate forces dynamically and avoid safety violations.

The approach yielded higher-quality task completion, such as an average peg insertion depth of 25.0 mm compared to 23.6 mm for the best baseline, and consistently maintained stable contact forces during interactions.
solution: A policy is trained purely in simulation to predict end-effector poses, gripper commands, contact states, and crucially, the desired *normal force direction* as a dynamics-invariant signal.
A force-aware admittance controller is deployed in the real world, dynamically integrating the policy's predictions with a manually tuned target normal force magnitude to provide adaptive compliance.

The controller selectively regulates forces and stiffness along task-relevant directions, enabling normal force regulation for stable contact and tangent stiffening to overcome resistive forces, based on the predicted contact state and force direction.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Zhejiang University developed a framework that enables effective sim-to-real transfer for contact-rich manipulation by learning desired contact force directions in simulation. The approach achieved an overall success rate of 91% across diverse tasks, outperforming baselines and reducing force-related failures through adaptive compliance.

title: Direction Matters: Learning Force Direction Enables Sim-to-Real Contact-Rich Manipulation

abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has achieved remarkable success in various robotic tasks; however, its deployment in real-world scenarios, particularly in contact-rich environments, often overlooks critical safety and stability aspects. Policies without passivity guarantees can result in system instability, posing risks to robots, their environments, and human operators. In this work, we investigate the limitations of traditional RL policies when deployed in contact-rich tasks and explore the combination of energy-based passive control with safe RL in both training and deployment to answer these challenges. Firstly, we reveal the discovery that standard RL policy does not satisfy stability in contact-rich scenarios. Secondly, we introduce a \textit{passivity-aware} RL policy training with energy-based constraints in our safe RL formulation. Lastly, a passivity filter is exerted on the policy output for \textit{passivity-ensured} control during deployment. We conduct comparative studies on a contact-rich robotic maze exploration task, evaluating the effects of learning passivity-aware policies and the importance of passivity-ensured control. The experiments demonstrate that a passivity-agnostic RL policy easily violates energy constraints in deployment, even though it achieves high task completion in training. The results show that our proposed approach guarantees control stability through passivity filtering and improves the energy efficiency through passivity-aware training. A video of real-world experiments is available as supplementary material. We also release the checkpoint model and offline data for pre-training at \href{this https URL}{Hugging Face}.
title: Passivity-Centric Safe Reinforcement Learning for Contact-Rich Robotic Tasks

abstract:
Reinforcement-learned locomotion enables legged robots to perform highly
dynamic motions but often accompanies time-consuming manual tuning of joint
stiffness. This paper introduces a novel control paradigm that integrates
variable stiffness into the action space alongside joint positions, enabling
grouped stiffness control such as per-joint stiffness (PJS), per-leg stiffness
(PLS) and hybrid joint-leg stiffness (HJLS). We show that variable stiffness
policies, with grouping in per-leg stiffness (PLS), outperform position-based
control in velocity tracking and push recovery. In contrast, HJLS excels in
energy efficiency. Despite the fact that our policy is trained on flat floor
only, our method showcases robust walking behaviour on diverse outdoor
terrains, indicating robust sim-to-real transfer. Our approach simplifies
design by eliminating per-joint stiffness tuning while keeping competitive
results with various metrics.
title: Variable Stiffness for Robust Locomotion through Reinforcement Learning

abstract:
Controlling contact forces during interactions is critical for locomotion and
manipulation tasks. While sim-to-real reinforcement learning (RL) has succeeded
in many contact-rich problems, current RL methods achieve forceful interactions
implicitly without explicitly regulating forces. We propose a method for
training RL policies for direct force control without requiring access to force
sensing. We showcase our method on a whole-body control platform of a quadruped
robot with an arm. Such force control enables us to perform gravity
compensation and impedance control, unlocking compliant whole-body
manipulation. The learned whole-body controller with variable compliance makes
it intuitive for humans to teleoperate the robot by only commanding the
manipulator, and the robot's body adjusts automatically to achieve the desired
position and force. Consequently, a human teleoperator can easily demonstrate a
wide variety of loco-manipulation tasks. To the best of our knowledge, we
provide the first deployment of learned whole-body force control in legged
manipulators, paving the way for more versatile and adaptable legged robots.

keyInsights: Direct, user-commandable force control can be effectively learned for whole-body legged manipulators using only proprioceptive sensing and simulated external force interactions.
A unified policy enables seamless coordination of the robot's legs and arm, allowing for efficient force application, expanded workspace, and compliant behavior.

The learned force control inherently supports capabilities like impedance control, gravity compensation, and robust compliance to disturbances, enhancing human-robot interaction and kinesthetic teaching.
originalProblem: Existing reinforcement learning methods for contact-rich tasks achieve forceful interactions implicitly, lacking direct control or regulation of interaction forces.
Classical force control techniques often rely on precise force-torque sensors or complex inverse dynamics models, which are not always available or practical for legged manipulators.
The absence of explicit, user-commandable force control limits legged robots' versatility, safety, and adaptability for tasks requiring compliant interaction or direct force application.
results: The policy achieved real-world end-effector force tracking with a 5-10 N error across a 0-70 N range and demonstrated the application of pulling forces up to 90 N, exceeding the arm's rated payload.
Successfully implemented impedance control and fully compliant gravity compensation, allowing for kinesthetic teaching and robust interaction with objects up to 4 kg without additional training.
The whole-body position control policy expanded the manipulation workspace volume by 59% compared to a fixed-base arm, with real-world average positional errors around 4-7 cm.
solution: A unified sim-to-real reinforcement learning policy is trained for a quadruped robot with a mounted arm, capable of simultaneously handling locomotion, end-effector position, and force regulation.
The policy relies solely on proprioceptive sensing (joint encoders, IMU) and an estimated privileged state, explicitly avoiding external force-torque sensors.

Training involves exposing the robot to an external spring-damper force field in simulation, allowing the policy to learn force application and tracking by alternating between end-effector position and force command modes.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at the Improbable AI Lab at MIT developed a method to train reinforcement learning policies for direct whole-body force control in legged manipulators, eliminating the need for force-torque sensors. The system achieved real-world end-effector force tracking with 5-10 N error and allowed the robot to apply pulling forces up to 90 N, significantly extending its manipulation capabilities and enabling compliant interaction.

title: Learning Force Control for Legged Manipulation

abstract:
Compliance plays a crucial role in manipulation, as it balances between the
concurrent control of position and force under uncertainties. Yet compliance is
often overlooked by today's visuomotor policies that solely focus on position
control. This paper introduces Adaptive Compliance Policy (ACP), a novel
framework that learns to dynamically adjust system compliance both spatially
and temporally for given manipulation tasks from human demonstrations,
improving upon previous approaches that rely on pre-selected compliance
parameters or assume uniform constant stiffness. However, computing full
compliance parameters from human demonstrations is an ill-defined problem.
Instead, we estimate an approximate compliance profile with two useful
properties: avoiding large contact forces and encouraging accurate tracking.
Our approach enables robots to handle complex contact-rich manipulation tasks
and achieves over 50\% performance improvement compared to state-of-the-art
visuomotor policy methods. For result videos, see
this https URL

keyInsights: Approximating compliance by focusing on avoiding large internal forces and encouraging accurate tracking, rather than estimating exact stiffness, provides a practical and effective solution for learning from demonstrations.

A rule-based method for deriving approximate stiffness directions from human demonstrations (low stiffness along force, high stiffness orthogonal) effectively overcomes the ill-posed problem of full stiffness matrix estimation.

Sophisticated force/torque data encoding, specifically using Fast Fourier Transform (FFT), can significantly improve robustness and decision-making in tasks with dynamic and complex contact interactions.
originalProblem: State-of-the-art visuomotor policies primarily focus on position control, making them brittle and prone to failure in contact-rich manipulation tasks.
Optimal robot compliance is not static but dynamically varies throughout a task, a challenge current methods struggle to adapt to effectively.
Extracting a full, precise stiffness matrix (compliance information) from single human demonstrations is an ill-posed problem, limiting the practical transfer of human expertise.
results: ACP achieved over 50% performance improvement in contact-rich tasks like object flipping and vase wiping compared to baseline visuomotor policies with uniform compliance or position-only control.
For the item flipping task, ACP demonstrated a 96% success rate, significantly outperforming compliant (23%) and stiff (14%) baseline policies.
In the vase wiping task, ACP achieved a 93.75% success rate, with FFT encoding proving particularly beneficial and successfully avoiding catastrophic failures observed with stiff policies.
solution: Develops an Adaptive Compliance Policy (ACP) that learns an approximate compliance profile from human demonstrations, represented by a virtual target pose and stiffness magnitude.
Employs a novel stiffness direction rule that applies low stiffness along the direction of measured force feedback and high stiffness in orthogonal directions, ensuring practical interaction.
Integrates learned adaptive compliance into a diffusion-based visuomotor policy, utilizing multimodal inputs including visual data, proprioception, and a novel FFT encoding for force/torque data.
paper_summary.summary: The Adaptive Compliance Policy (ACP) framework enables robots to learn and dynamically adjust their mechanical compliance, both spatially and temporally, directly from human demonstrations. This approach leads to over 50% performance improvement in contact-rich manipulation tasks, such as object flipping and vase wiping, compared to existing position-focused visuomotor policies.

title: Adaptive Compliance Policy: Learning Approximate Compliance for  Diffusion Guided Control

abstract: Diffusion policies have shown impressive results in robot imitation learning, even for tasks that require satisfaction of kinematic equality constraints. However, task performance alone is not a reliable indicator of the policy's ability to precisely learn constraints in the training data. To investigate, we analyze how well diffusion policies discover these manifolds with a case study on a bimanual pick-and-place task that encourages fulfillment of a kinematic constraint for success. We study how three factors affect trained policies: dataset size, dataset quality, and manifold curvature. Our experiments show diffusion policies learn a coarse approximation of the constraint manifold with learning affected negatively by decreases in both dataset size and quality. On the other hand, the curvature of the constraint manifold showed inconclusive correlations with both constraint satisfaction and task success. A hardware evaluation verifies the applicability of our results in the real world. Project website with additional results and visuals: this https URL

organization_info.name: Computer Science and Artificial Intelligence Laboratory

keyInsights: Diffusion policies learn only a coarse approximation of kinematic constraint manifolds, consistently exhibiting measurable violations in predicted actions, even with optimal training data.

The quality of training data, specifically its adherence to kinematic constraints, proved to be a more critical factor for task success and constraint satisfaction than the sheer quantity of data, especially in low-data regimes.
Robust low-level controllers and physical compliance play a crucial role in mitigating the policy's predicted constraint violations, allowing tasks to succeed despite imperfect policy learning.
Manifold curvature, as measured by the Kretschmann scalar, did not show a significant correlation with a policy's ability to satisfy kinematic constraints or achieve task success.
originalProblem: It was unclear if diffusion policies truly learned to precisely satisfy kinematic constraints, or if their success in robotics was primarily due to robust low-level controllers or compliant hardware.
Task performance alone did not reliably indicate a policy's intrinsic ability to adhere to the underlying kinematic constraint manifolds.
Existing methods for handling constraints in generative models often faced limitations in tractability, strict guarantees, or tuning requirements.
results: Policies trained with 50 "clean" demonstrations achieved higher task success rates than those trained with 200 highly perturbed demonstrations.
The best-performing policy (trained on 200 clean demonstrations) still exhibited average predicted action errors of 1.22 cm in position and 1.80° in orientation relative to the observed state.

Hardware experiments corroborated simulation findings, with the policy achieving a 73% overall success rate while exhibiting similar constraint violations (0.81 cm, 1.04°) that were often masked by physical compliance.
Correlation coefficients between manifold curvature (Kretschmann scalar) and task success or constraint error were negligible, suggesting it was not a predictive measure in this context.
solution: A "transform-locking" teleoperation method was developed to generate high-quality, constraint-satisfying bimanual robot demonstrations for training.
Training datasets were systematically perturbed with controlled noise to investigate the impact of data quality on a policy's ability to learn and satisfy constraints.
Precise metrics were used to measure kinematic constraint violation directly in the policy's predicted actions, isolating learning ability from execution effects, and manifold curvature was computed.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at MIT CSAIL analyzed the intrinsic ability of diffusion policies to learn kinematic constraint manifolds, revealing that these policies achieve only a coarse approximation of constraints. Their findings emphasize that training data quality is more crucial than quantity for robust performance in constrained robot tasks, particularly with limited datasets.

title: How Well do Diffusion Policies Learn Kinematic Constraint Manifolds?

abstract: In this paper, we review the question of which action space is best suited for controlling a real biped robot in combination with Sim2Real training. Position control has been popular as it has been shown to be more sample efficient and intuitive to combine with other planning algorithms. However, for position control gain tuning is required to achieve the best possible policy performance. We show that instead, using a torque-based action space enables task-and-robot agnostic learning with less parameter tuning and mitigates the sim-to-reality gap by taking advantage of torque control's inherent compliance. Also, we accelerate the torque-based-policy training process by pre-training the policy to remain upright by compensating for gravity. The paper showcases the first successful sim-to-real transfer of a torque-based deep reinforcement learning policy on a real human-sized biped robot. The video is available at this https URL.
title: Torque-based Deep Reinforcement Learning for Task-and-Robot Agnostic Learning on Bipedal Robots Using Sim-to-Real Transfer

abstract:
Optimal Control for legged robots has gone through a paradigm shift from
position-based to torque-based control, owing to the latter's compliant and
robust nature. In parallel to this shift, the community has also turned to Deep
Reinforcement Learning (DRL) as a promising approach to directly learn
locomotion policies for complex real-life tasks. However, most end-to-end DRL
approaches still operate in position space, mainly because learning in torque
space is often sample-inefficient and does not consistently converge to natural
gaits. To address these challenges, we propose a two-stage framework. In the
first stage, we generate our own imitation data by training a position-based
policy, eliminating the need for expert knowledge to design optimal
controllers. The second stage incorporates decaying action priors, a novel
method to enhance the exploration of torque-based policies aided by imitation
rewards. We show that our approach consistently outperforms imitation learning
alone and is robust to scaling these rewards from 0.1x to 10x. We further
validate the benefits of torque control by comparing the robustness of a
position-based policy to a position-assisted torque-based policy on a quadruped
(Unitree Go1) without any domain randomization in the form of external
disturbances during training.
title: DecAP: Decaying Action Priors for Accelerated Imitation Learning of  Torque-Based Legged Locomotion Policies

abstract: Proprioceptive information is critical for precise servo control by providing real-time robotic states. Its collaboration with vision is highly expected to enhance performances of the manipulation policy in complex tasks. However, recent studies have reported inconsistent observations on the generalization of vision-proprioception policies. In this work, we investigate this by conducting temporally controlled experiments. We found that during task sub-phases that robot's motion transitions, which require target localization, the vision modality of the vision-proprioception policy plays a limited role. Further analysis reveals that the policy naturally gravitates toward concise proprioceptive signals that offer faster loss reduction when training, thereby dominating the optimization and suppressing the learning of the visual modality during motion-transition phases. To alleviate this, we propose the Gradient Adjustment with Phase-guidance (GAP) algorithm that adaptively modulates the optimization of proprioception, enabling dynamic collaboration within the vision-proprioception policy. Specifically, we leverage proprioception to capture robotic states and estimate the probability of each timestep in the trajectory belonging to motion-transition phases. During policy learning, we apply fine-grained adjustment that reduces the magnitude of proprioception's gradient based on estimated probabilities, leading to robust and generalizable vision-proprioception policies. The comprehensive experiments demonstrate GAP is applicable in both simulated and real-world environments, across one-arm and dual-arm setups, and compatible with both conventional and Vision-Language-Action models. We believe this work can offer valuable insights into the development of vision-proprioception policies in robotic manipulation.

organization_info.name: Beijing Key Laboratory of Research on Large Models and Intelligent Governance

keyInsights: Vision-proprioception policies frequently fail during specific 'motion-transition phases' of a task, where accurate target localization is crucial, due to underutilization of the visual modality.

The vision modality is suppressed during training because policies 'gravitate toward' concise proprioceptive signals, which offer faster loss reduction, thereby dominating optimization and hindering the learning of subtle visual cues.

Dynamically adjusting the optimization process by reducing the influence of proprioceptive gradients during critical motion-transition phases can effectively prevent visual suppression and foster robust multimodal learning.
originalProblem: Inconsistent empirical observations regarding the efficacy of vision-proprioception policies in robotic manipulation, with some studies reporting improvements and others showing detrimental effects compared to vision-only approaches.
A lack of clear understanding about *when* and *why* vision-proprioception policies succeed or fail, despite the intuitive benefit of combining modalities.
Existing explanations like overfitting or causal confusion do not fully account for the observed discrepancies in multimodal policy performance.
results: GAP-equipped policies consistently outperformed vision-only and standard fusion baselines, achieving superior success rates (e.g., 94.2% in Meta-World pick-place) across various simulated and real-world tasks.

Intervention experiments and Out-of-Distribution (OOD) generalization tests demonstrated that GAP enhances the utilization of the vision modality, reducing performance degradation during transition phases and improving robustness to visual variations.

The GAP algorithm proved versatile, improving performance across different policy architectures (MLP, diffusion, transformer) and with Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models like Octo, showing an average 17% improvement on Octo-VP models.
solution: The authors formally analyze vision-proprioception policies under the Behavior Cloning paradigm, focusing on gradient dynamics during training.
The Gradient Adjustment with Phase-guidance (GAP) algorithm is proposed, which estimates 'motion-transition phases' using Change Point Detection and a temporal network.

GAP adaptively modulates the learning rate for proprioceptive feature parameters, reducing their gradient magnitude during motion-transition phases to prevent visual modality suppression and encourage better collaboration.
paper_summary.summary: This research investigates the reasons behind the inconsistent performance of vision-proprioception policies in robotic manipulation, identifying that the visual modality is often suppressed during motion-transition phases. The Gradient Adjustment with Phase-guidance (GAP) algorithm is introduced to adaptively modulate optimization, enhancing collaboration between modalities and leading to more robust and generalizable policies.

title: When would Vision-Proprioception Policies Fail in Robotic Manipulation?

abstract:
Dexterous manipulation has seen remarkable progress in recent years, with
policies capable of executing many complex and contact-rich tasks in
simulation. However, transferring these policies from simulation to real world
remains a significant challenge. One important issue is the mismatch in
low-level controller dynamics, where identical trajectories can lead to vastly
different contact forces and behaviors when control parameters vary. Existing
approaches often rely on manual tuning or controller randomization, which can
be labor-intensive, task-specific, and introduce significant training
difficulty. In this work, we propose a framework that jointly learns actions
and controller parameters based on the historical information of both
trajectory and controller. This adaptive controller adjustment mechanism allows
the policy to automatically tune control parameters during execution, thereby
mitigating the sim-to-real gap without extensive manual tuning or excessive
randomization. Moreover, by explicitly providing controller parameters as part
of the observation, our approach facilitates better reasoning over force
interactions and improves robustness in real-world scenarios. Experimental
results demonstrate that our method achieves improved transfer performance
across a variety of dexterous tasks involving variable force conditions.

keyInsights: Mismatch in robot controller behavior is a critical but often overlooked factor in the sim-to-real gap
Adaptive controller parameter prediction is crucial for robust real-world performance
Controller parameters systematically adapt to object properties (e.g., increased stiffness for heavier objects)
originalProblem: Transferring policies from simulation to real robots is challenging due to discrepancies in low-level controller dynamics
Manual tuning of controller parameters is labor-intensive and task-specific while domain randomization can increase training difficulty
results: Outperformed manual tuning baseline in simulation and achieved successful transfer to real robot without additional training
Demonstrated robust performance across objects with varying physical parameters
Showed significantly better performance than baseline without controller adaptation, especially in real-world experiments
solution: DexCtrl framework that learns to predict both actions and controller parameters adaptively based on historical information
Two-module architecture using self-attention for action prediction and cross-attention for controller parameter prediction
Training an oracle policy in simulation and distilling into a student policy for real-world deployment
paper_summary.summary: A novel adaptive controller learning framework enables successful sim-to-real transfer of dexterous manipulation policies by jointly predicting actions and controller parameters based on historical trajectories, demonstrating improved performance on in-hand object rotation and flipping tasks using a LEAP robotic hand while reducing the need for manual parameter tuning.

title: DexCtrl: Towards Sim-to-Real Dexterity with Adaptive Controller Learning

abstract:
The capability to adapt compliance by varying muscle stiffness is crucial for
dexterous manipulation skills in humans. Incorporating compliance in robot
motor control is crucial to performing real-world force interaction tasks with
human-level dexterity. This work presents a Deep Model Predictive Variable
Impedance Controller for compliant robotic manipulation which combines Variable
Impedance Control with Model Predictive Control (MPC). A generalized Cartesian
impedance model of a robot manipulator is learned using an exploration strategy
maximizing the information gain. This model is used within an MPC framework to
adapt the impedance parameters of a low-level variable impedance controller to
achieve the desired compliance behavior for different manipulation tasks
without any retraining or finetuning. The deep Model Predictive Variable
Impedance Control approach is evaluated using a Franka Emika Panda robotic
manipulator operating on different manipulation tasks in simulations and real
experiments. The proposed approach was compared with model-free and model-based
reinforcement approaches in variable impedance control for transferability
between tasks and performance.

organization_info.name: Norwegian University of Science and Technology (NTNU)

title: Deep Model Predictive Variable Impedance Control

abstract: In recent years, on-policy reinforcement learning (RL) has been successfully applied to many different continuous control tasks. While RL algorithms are often conceptually simple, their state-of-the-art implementations take numerous low- and high-level design decisions that strongly affect the performance of the resulting agents. Those choices are usually not extensively discussed in the literature, leading to discrepancy between published descriptions of algorithms and their implementations. This makes it hard to attribute progress in RL and slows down overall progress [Engstrom'20]. As a step towards filling that gap, we implement >50 such ``choices'' in a unified on-policy RL framework, allowing us to investigate their impact in a large-scale empirical study. We train over 250'000 agents in five continuous control environments of different complexity and provide insights and practical recommendations for on-policy training of RL agents.

keyInsights: Policy initialization, by scaling final policy layer weights down (e.g., to 0.01) and ensuring zero-mean, low-standard-deviation initial actions, dramatically improves training performance and robustness.
Tanh activations consistently outperform ReLU in network architectures for on-policy reinforcement learning, challenging common deep learning practices.
Observation normalization, Generalized Advantage Estimation (GAE) with a lambda around 0.9, and tanh-based action transformations are crucial implementation choices for achieving robust performance.
originalProblem: The deep reinforcement learning community faces a reproducibility crisis, where it is difficult to replicate reported results.
Performance gains in deep RL are hard to attribute definitively to algorithmic innovations versus subtle, undocumented implementation details.
Complex codebases with numerous unstated design decisions create barriers to progress and hinder the development of best practices.
results: PPO consistently outperformed other policy losses (AWR, PG, RPA, V-MPO, V-Trace) across four of five continuous control environments.
Careful policy initialization, specifically scaling final policy layer weights to 0.01, improved performance by 66% on the Humanoid environment.
Separate policy and value networks generally outperformed shared architectures, and tanh activations yielded better performance than ReLU in the tested environments.
solution: Developed a unified, configurable on-policy learning framework built on SEED RL and PPO, exposing over 50 design choices as configurable options.
Conducted a systematic empirical study, training over 250,000 agents across five continuous control environments to evaluate implementation choices.
Employed a thematic grouping strategy for choices and utilized conditional 95th percentile and distribution analyses to identify critical factors within competitive configurations.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Google Research conducted a large-scale empirical study on on-policy reinforcement learning, evaluating over 50 implementation choices across 250,000 agents to identify critical factors influencing performance and provide evidence-based best practices. The study found that carefully chosen policy initialization and tanh activations are crucial for robust learning, while PPO consistently outperforms other policy losses.

title: What Matters In On-Policy Reinforcement Learning? A Large-Scale Empirical Study

abstract: Simulation parameter settings such as contact models and object geometry approximations are critical to training robust robotic policies capable of transferring from simulation to real-world deployment. Previous approaches typically handcraft distributions over such parameters (domain randomization), or identify parameters that best match the dynamics of the real environment (system identification). However, there is often an irreducible gap between simulation and reality: attempting to match the dynamics between simulation and reality across all states and tasks may be infeasible and may not lead to policies that perform well in reality for a specific task. Addressing this issue, we propose AdaptSim, a new task-driven adaptation framework for sim-to-real transfer that aims to optimize task performance in target (real) environments -- instead of matching dynamics between simulation and reality. First, we meta-learn an adaptation policy in simulation using reinforcement learning for adjusting the simulation parameter distribution based on the current policy's performance in a target environment. We then perform iterative real-world adaptation by inferring new simulation parameter distributions for policy training, using a small amount of real data. We perform experiments in three robotic tasks: (1) swing-up of linearized double pendulum, (2) dynamic table-top pushing of a bottle, and (3) dynamic scooping of food pieces with a spatula. Our extensive simulation and hardware experiments demonstrate AdaptSim achieving 1-3x asymptotic performance and $\sim$2x real data efficiency when adapting to different environments, compared to methods based on Sys-ID and directly training the task policy in target environments. Website: this https URL
title: AdaptSim: Task-Driven Simulation Adaptation for Sim-to-Real Transfer

abstract:
Shifting from traditional control strategies to Deep Reinforcement Learning
(RL) for legged robots poses inherent challenges, especially when addressing
real-world physical constraints during training. While high-fidelity
simulations provide significant benefits, they often bypass these essential
physical limitations. In this paper, we experiment with the Constrained Markov
Decision Process (CMDP) framework instead of the conventional unconstrained RL
for robotic applications. We perform a comparative study of different
constrained policy optimization algorithms to identify suitable methods for
practical implementation. Our robot experiments demonstrate the critical role
of incorporating physical constraints, yielding successful sim-to-real
transfers, and reducing operational errors on physical systems. The CMDP
formulation streamlines the training process by separately handling constraints
from rewards. Our findings underscore the potential of constrained RL for the
effective development and deployment of learned controllers in robotics.
title: Evaluation of Constrained Reinforcement Learning Algorithms for Legged  Locomotion

abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) often necessitates a meticulous Markov Decision Process (MDP) design tailored to each task. This work aims to address this challenge by proposing a systematic approach to behavior synthesis and control for multi-contact loco-manipulation tasks, such as navigating spring-loaded doors and manipulating heavy dishwashers. We define a task-independent MDP to train RL policies using only a single demonstration per task generated from a model-based trajectory optimizer. Our approach incorporates an adaptive phase dynamics formulation to robustly track the demonstrations while accommodating dynamic uncertainties and external disturbances. We compare our method against prior motion imitation RL works and show that the learned policies achieve higher success rates across all considered tasks. These policies learn recovery maneuvers that are not present in the demonstration, such as re-grasping objects during execution or dealing with slippages. Finally, we successfully transfer the policies to a real robot, demonstrating the practical viability of our approach.
title: Guided Reinforcement Learning for Robust Multi-Contact Loco-Manipulation

abstract:
Learning-based approaches, particularly reinforcement learning (RL), have
become widely used for developing control policies for autonomous agents, such
as locomotion policies for legged robots. RL training typically maximizes a
predefined reward (or minimizes a corresponding cost/loss) by iteratively
optimizing policies within a simulator. Starting from a randomly initialized
policy, the empirical expected reward follows a trajectory with an overall
increasing trend. While some policies become temporarily stuck in local optima,
a well-defined training process generally converges to a reward level with
noisy oscillations. However, selecting a policy for real-world deployment is
rarely an analytical decision (i.e., simply choosing the one with the highest
reward) and is instead often performed through trial and error. To improve
sim-to-real transfer, most research focuses on the pre-convergence stage,
employing techniques such as domain randomization, multi-fidelity training,
adversarial training, and architectural innovations. However, these methods do
not eliminate the inevitable convergence trajectory and noisy oscillations of
rewards, leading to heuristic policy selection or cherry-picking. This paper
addresses the post-convergence sim-to-real transfer problem by introducing a
worst-case performance transference optimization approach, formulated as a
convex quadratic-constrained linear programming problem. Extensive experiments
demonstrate its effectiveness in transferring RL-based locomotion policies from
simulation to real-world laboratory tests.
title: Post-Convergence Sim-to-Real Policy Transfer: A Principled Alternative  to Cherry-Picking

abstract: In recent years, increasing attention has been directed to leveraging pre-trained vision models for motor control. While existing works mainly emphasize the importance of this pre-training phase, the arguably equally important role played by downstream policy learning during control-specific fine-tuning is often neglected. It thus remains unclear if pre-trained vision models are consistent in their effectiveness under different control policies. To bridge this gap in understanding, we conduct a comprehensive study on 14 pre-trained vision models using 3 distinct classes of policy learning methods, including reinforcement learning (RL), imitation learning through behavior cloning (BC), and imitation learning with a visual reward function (VRF). Our study yields a series of intriguing results, including the discovery that the effectiveness of pre-training is highly dependent on the choice of the downstream policy learning algorithm. We show that conventionally accepted evaluation based on RL methods is highly variable and therefore unreliable, and further advocate for using more robust methods like VRF and BC. To facilitate more universal evaluations of pre-trained models and their policy learning methods in the future, we also release a benchmark of 21 tasks across 3 different environments alongside our work.
title: For Pre-Trained Vision Models in Motor Control, Not All Policy Learning Methods are Created Equal

abstract:
This study explores a learning-based tri-finger robotic arm manipulating
task, which requires complex movements and coordination among the fingers. By
employing reinforcement learning, we train an agent to acquire the necessary
skills for proficient manipulation. To enhance the efficiency and effectiveness
of the learning process, two knowledge transfer strategies, fine-tuning and
curriculum learning, were utilized within the soft actor-critic architecture.
Fine-tuning allows the agent to leverage pre-trained knowledge and adapt it to
new tasks. Several variations like model transfer, policy transfer, and
across-task transfer were implemented and evaluated. To eliminate the need for
pretraining, curriculum learning decomposes the advanced task into simpler,
progressive stages, mirroring how humans learn. The number of learning stages,
the context of the sub-tasks, and the transition timing were found to be the
critical design parameters. The key factors of two learning strategies and
corresponding effects were explored in context-aware and context-unaware
scenarios, enabling us to identify the scenarios where the methods demonstrate
optimal performance, derive conclusive insights, and contribute to a broader
range of learning-based engineering applications.
title: Exploring CausalWorld: Enhancing robotic manipulation via knowledge  transfer and curriculum learning

abstract:
Stable and robust robotic grasping is essential for current and future robot
applications. In recent works, the use of large datasets and supervised
learning has enhanced speed and precision in antipodal grasping. However, these
methods struggle with perception and calibration errors due to large planning
horizons. To obtain more robust and reactive grasping motions, leveraging
reinforcement learning combined with tactile sensing is a promising direction.
Yet, there is no systematic evaluation of how the complexity of force-based
tactile sensing affects the learning behavior for grasping tasks. This paper
compares various tactile and environmental setups using two model-free
reinforcement learning approaches for antipodal grasping. Our findings suggest
that under imperfect visual perception, various tactile features improve
learning outcomes, while complex tactile inputs complicate training.

organization_info.name: Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems

keyInsights: Tactile sensing proves most beneficial when visual perception is imperfect, particularly for correcting calibration errors, rather than under ideal visual conditions.
For 2-finger antipodal grippers, a global understanding of force via aggregated force vectors is more crucial for learning robust grasps than high spatial resolution of tactile data.
Simple tactile force vector information consistently improves the generalization capabilities of learned policies to grasp novel or previously unseen objects.
originalProblem: Achieving stable and robust robotic grasping is challenging, especially for 2-finger antipodal grippers which have limited in-hand manipulation capabilities and provide sparse rewards for reinforcement learning.
Existing vision-centric grasping methods struggle to react to real-time perturbations, perception errors, or unexpected environmental changes.
There was a lack of systematic evaluation on how the complexity of force-based tactile sensing (area, resolution, measured quantities) affects the learning behavior for 2-finger antipodal grasping.
results: Under perfect visual perception, tactile sensing did not significantly improve in-distribution grasping success rates, with all setups achieving similar performance levels (~60%).

Tactile force vector information (V and VK) significantly improved performance and reduced variance under visual offset noise (calibration errors), outperforming binary or magnitude-only tactile data.

Policies trained with simple tactile input (V) maintained more consistent performance across 10-100 diverse objects and showed higher success rates on unseen objects compared to policies without tactile input in sim-to-real experiments, where vision-only policies often exhibited near-zero success rates.
solution: The authors developed a comprehensive reinforcement learning framework using two model-free algorithms (SAC and MPO) in the high-fidelity NVIDIA Isaac Gym simulation environment.

They designed a novel simulated tactile sensor approximation, modeled after their real-world Minsight sensor, to systematically vary tactile sensing properties such as sensing area (single unit, K-feature) and measured quantities (binary, magnitude, 3D force vector).

Extensive experiments were conducted comparing various tactile configurations under different visual conditions (perfect, noisy, low-resolution images), object diversities, and validated through sim-to-real transfer on a Franka Panda robot with Minsight sensors.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at the University of Tübingen and MPI-IS systematically evaluated how different force-based tactile sensing configurations impact learning robust 2-finger antipodal grasping via reinforcement learning. Their findings indicate that tactile force vector information is crucial for compensating for visual imperfections and improving generalization to novel objects.

title: The Role of Tactile Sensing for Learning Reach and Grasp

abstract:
This paper presents a data-driven strategy to streamline the deployment of
model-based controllers in legged robotic hardware platforms. Our approach
leverages a model-free safe learning algorithm to automate the tuning of
control gains, addressing the mismatch between the simplified model used in the
control formulation and the real system. This method substantially mitigates
the risk of hazardous interactions with the robot by sample-efficiently
optimizing parameters within a probably safe region. Additionally, we extend
the applicability of our approach to incorporate the different gait parameters
as contexts, leading to a safe, sample-efficient exploration algorithm capable
of tuning a motion controller for diverse gait patterns. We validate our method
through simulation and hardware experiments, where we demonstrate that the
algorithm obtains superior performance on tuning a model-based motion
controller for multiple gaits safely.
title: Tuning Legged Locomotion Controllers via Safe Bayesian Optimization

abstract: Generalist robot manipulation policies (GMPs) have the potential to generalize across a wide range of tasks, devices, and environments. However, existing policies continue to struggle with out-of-distribution scenarios due to the inherent difficulty of collecting sufficient action data to cover extensively diverse domains. While fine-tuning offers a practical way to quickly adapt a GMPs to novel domains and tasks with limited samples, we observe that the performance of the resulting GMPs differs significantly with respect to the design choices of fine-tuning strategies. In this work, we first conduct an in-depth empirical study to investigate the effect of key factors in GMPs fine-tuning strategies, covering the action space, policy head, supervision signal and the choice of tunable parameters, where 2,500 rollouts are evaluated for a single configuration. We systematically discuss and summarize our findings and identify the key design choices, which we believe give a practical guideline for GMPs fine-tuning. We observe that in a low-data regime, with carefully chosen fine-tuning strategies, a GMPs significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art imitation learning algorithms. The results presented in this work establish a new baseline for future studies on fine-tuned GMPs, and provide a significant addition to the GMPs toolbox for the community.

organization_info.name: Commonwealth Scientific and Industrial Research Organisation (CSIRO)

keyInsights: Delta Joint Position action space consistently outperforms end-effector representations for fine-tuning generalist robot manipulation policies (GMPs).
Simpler Linear Heads demonstrate superior performance over more complex Diffusion Heads during fine-tuning, contrary to expectations.
Updating all model parameters (full-tuning), particularly the vision encoder, is crucial for optimal fine-tuning performance, indicating the importance of visual domain adaptation.
originalProblem: Pre-trained generalist robot manipulation policies often struggle to generalize effectively to out-of-distribution scenarios without additional adaptation.
Prior fine-tuning research suffered from limited scope, inconsistent evaluation practices, fixed data quantities, and a lack of systematic baseline comparisons.
Uncertainty existed regarding optimal choices for action space representation, policy head architecture, and parameter tuning strategies for adapting GMPs.
results: The study identified a robust optimal fine-tuning configuration: Delta Joint Position action space, Linear Head policy architecture, and Full Parameter Tuning.
